title: Transformer Architecture — Attention Is All You Need Deep Dive
description: The original Transformer (Vaswani et al., 2017) is the foundation of every modern LLM. At Staff level, you must understand the encoder-decoder architecture, attention variants, and why the field converged on decoder-only. This is the prerequisite for all Gen AI topics.

sections.title: The Original Transformer: Encoder-Decoder at a Glance
sections.description:
psychology Intuition: The original Transformer translates sequences (e.g., English → French). The encoder reads the entire input sequence with bidirectional attention (each token sees all other tokens). The decoder generates the output one token at a time with causal attention (each token sees only itself and earlier tokens) plus cross-attention to the encoder output.

bar_chart Concrete numbers: Transformer-Base (used in the paper): 6 encoder + 6 decoder layers, d_model=512, d_ff=2048, h=8 attention heads, total ~65M params. Transformer-Big: 6+6 layers, d_model=1024, d_ff=4096, h=16 heads, ~213M params. BLEU score on WMT 2014 English→German: 28.4 (Big: 28.7), beating all previous RNN/CNN models by 2+ BLEU. Training: 8× P100 GPUs, 3.5 days for Base, 5 days for Big.

Key innovation: Self-attention replaced recurrence. O(1) path length between any two positions in the sequence (vs O(N) for RNNs). This parallelizes across all positions — training is O(N²) compute but fully parallel, unlike RNN's sequential O(N).
sections.codeBlock:
# Minimal Transformer encoder block
import torch, torch.nn as nn, math

class TransformerEncoderBlock(nn.Module):
    def __init__(self, d_model, n_heads, d_ff, dropout=0.1):
        super().__init__()
        self.self_attn = nn.MultiheadAttention(d_model, n_heads, dropout=dropout, batch_first=True)
        self.ffn = nn.Sequential(
            nn.Linear(d_model, d_ff),
            nn.ReLU(),
            nn.Dropout(dropout),
            nn.Linear(d_ff, d_model),
            nn.Dropout(dropout),
        )
        self.norm1 = nn.LayerNorm(d_model)
        self.norm2 = nn.LayerNorm(d_model)

    def forward(self, x, mask=None):
        # Self-attention with residual + post-norm (original paper style)
        x = self.norm1(x + self.self_attn(x, x, x, attn_mask=mask, need_weights=False)[0])
        x = self.norm2(x + self.ffn(x))
        return x

class TransformerDecoderBlock(nn.Module):
    def __init__(self, d_model, n_heads, d_ff, dropout=0.1):
        super().__init__()
        self.self_attn = nn.MultiheadAttention(d_model, n_heads, dropout=dropout, batch_first=True)
        self.cross_attn = nn.MultiheadAttention(d_model, n_heads, dropout=dropout, batch_first=True)
        self.ffn = nn.Sequential(
            nn.Linear(d_model, d_ff), nn.ReLU(),
            nn.Dropout(dropout), nn.Linear(d_ff, d_model), nn.Dropout(dropout),
        )
        self.norm1 = nn.LayerNorm(d_model)
        self.norm2 = nn.LayerNorm(d_model)
        self.norm3 = nn.LayerNorm(d_model)

    def forward(self, x, encoder_out, self_mask=None, cross_mask=None):
        # Causal self-attention + cross-attention + FFN
        x = self.norm1(x + self.self_attn(x, x, x, attn_mask=self_mask, need_weights=False)[0])
        x = self.norm2(x + self.cross_attn(x, encoder_out, encoder_out, attn_mask=cross_mask, need_weights=False)[0])
        x = self.norm3(x + self.ffn(x))
        return x

sections.title: Scaled Dot-Product Attention — The Core Equation
sections.description:
psychology Intuition: Attention computes a weighted sum of values, where weights are determined by the similarity between queries and keys. The scaling factor √d_k prevents the dot products from growing too large (which pushes softmax into regions with vanishingly small gradients).

The equation: Attention(Q,K,V) = softmax(QKᵀ / √d_k) · V. Each query vector Q_i compares against all key vectors K_j. The resulting weights determine how much of each value V_j to include in the output.

bar_chart Concrete numbers: For d_k=64 (Transformer-Base): dot product variance = d_k = 64 (assuming unit variance inputs), standard deviation = 8. Without scaling, max softmax input ≈ 3×8 = 24 → softmax outputs become nearly one-hot (max probability > 0.999). Gradient of softmax cross-entropy for one-hot targets: vanishing. With scaling by √64 = 8: inputs stay within [-3, 3] → healthy gradient flow.

search Interview Angle: "Why divide by √d_k in attention?" Derive: if Q and K are independent with mean 0, variance 1, then Q·K has mean 0, variance d_k. Softmax with large variance collapses to near-one-hot. Dividing by √d_k normalizes variance to 1, keeping the softmax regime well-conditioned for gradient flow.
sections.codeBlock:
# Scaled dot-product attention
import torch.nn.functional as F

def scaled_dot_product_attention(Q, K, V, mask=None):
    """
    Q: [B, H, T, d_k]
    K: [B, H, S, d_k]
    V: [B, H, S, d_v]
    """
    d_k = Q.size(-1)
    scores = torch.matmul(Q, K.transpose(-2, -1)) / math.sqrt(d_k)
    # scores shape: [B, H, T, S]

    if mask is not None:
        scores = scores.masked_fill(mask == 0, float('-inf'))

    attn_weights = F.softmax(scores, dim=-1)
    # Dropout on attention weights (applied in training)
    attn_weights = F.dropout(attn_weights, p=0.1, training=True)

    output = torch.matmul(attn_weights, V)
    return output, attn_weights

# Illustration: why √d_k scaling matters
import numpy as np

def softmax_histogram(d_k, scale=True):
    """Show softmax distribution before vs after scaling."""
    q = np.random.randn(128, d_k)
    k = np.random.randn(128, d_k)
    scores = q @ k.T
    if scale:
        scores /= math.sqrt(d_k)
    max_prob = np.max(F.softmax(torch.tensor(scores), dim=-1).numpy(), axis=1)
    return max_prob.mean()

print(f"Unscaled avg max prob: {softmax_histogram(64, scale=False):.4f}")
print(f"Scaled   avg max prob: {softmax_histogram(64, scale=True):.4f}")

sections.title: Multi-Head Attention — Ensemble of Subspaces
sections.description:
psychology Intuition: Single-head attention averages information across all dimensions. Multi-head attention splits the d_model into h heads, each attending to a different representational subspace. This lets the model jointly attend to information from different positions and different representation spaces (syntactic, semantic, positional).

Mechanism: Project Q, K, V into h sets of lower-dimensional vectors (d_k = d_v = d_model / h). Compute attention per head independently. Concatenate outputs and project back to d_model.

bar_chart Concrete numbers: Transformer-Base: h=8, d_k=64, d_v=64, d_model=512. Total attention FLOPs = 2 × T² × h × d_k ≈ 2 × T² × d_model (same as single-head). Transformer-Big: h=16, d_k=64. Each head captures different patterns: probing studies show some heads attend to syntax (next token, same POS), others to semantics (coreference, entity relations). Typical result: 30-50% of heads can be pruned without quality loss (head importance highly skewed).

search Interview Question: "Does multi-head attention add compute cost vs single-head?" No — the total FLOPs are identical because d_model is split across h heads. Single-head: QKᵀ = (d_model × d_model) attention. Multi-head: h × (d_k × d_k) attentions where d_k = d_model/h. Total: h × (d_model/h)² = d_model²/h — wait, that's less per head. Actually the cost is: each head projects Q, K, V from d_model to d_k (3 × d_model × d_k params). Total projections: h × 3 × d_model × d_k = 3 × d_model². Then attention: h × (T² × d_k + T × d_k²) ≈ h × T² × d_k = T² × d_model. Same as single head: T² × d_model.
sections.codeBlock:
# Multi-Head Attention implementation
class MultiHeadAttention(nn.Module):
    def __init__(self, d_model, n_heads, dropout=0.1):
        super().__init__()
        assert d_model % n_heads == 0
        self.n_heads = n_heads
        self.d_k = d_model // n_heads

        self.wq = nn.Linear(d_model, d_model, bias=False)
        self.wk = nn.Linear(d_model, d_model, bias=False)
        self.wv = nn.Linear(d_model, d_model, bias=False)
        self.wo = nn.Linear(d_model, d_model, bias=False)
        self.dropout = nn.Dropout(dropout)

    def forward(self, q, k, v, mask=None):
        B, T, D = q.shape

        # [B, T, D] -> [B, T, n_heads, d_k] -> [B, n_heads, T, d_k]
        Q = self.wq(q).view(B, T, self.n_heads, self.d_k).transpose(1, 2)
        K = self.wk(k).view(B, T, self.n_heads, self.d_k).transpose(1, 2)
        V = self.wv(v).view(B, T, self.n_heads, self.d_k).transpose(1, 2)

        # Attention: [B, n_heads, T, T]
        scores = Q @ K.transpose(-2, -1) / math.sqrt(self.d_k)
        if mask is not None:
            scores = scores.masked_fill(mask == 0, float('-inf'))
        attn = self.dropout(F.softmax(scores, dim=-1))

        # [B, n_heads, T, d_k] -> [B, T, n_heads, d_k] -> [B, T, D]
        out = (attn @ V).transpose(1, 2).contiguous().view(B, T, D)
        return self.wo(out), attn

sections.title: Positional Encoding — Sinusoidal vs Learned
sections.description:
psychology Intuition: Self-attention is permutation-invariant — shuffling tokens produces the same output. Positional encoding adds a unique signal per position so the model knows token order. The original Transformer used sinusoidal encoding: a fixed function using sin/cos at different frequencies. Each dimension i of the position vector follows: PE(pos, 2i) = sin(pos/10000^(2i/d_model)), PE(pos, 2i+1) = cos(pos/10000^(2i/d_model)).

bar_chart Concrete numbers: Sinusoidal encoding: 4×(d_model/2) unique values across all positions (since sin/cos cycle). Wavelengths range from 2π (dim 0, highest frequency) to 2π × 10000 (dim d_model-2, lowest frequency). This creates a unique fingerprint per position that the attention mechanism can learn to decode. Key property: any linear transformation of PE(pos+k) can be expressed as a function of PE(pos) — the model can learn relative position biases from the fixed sinusoidal pattern. Modern LLMs use RoPE (see LLM Architecture) instead, which combines position info directly into the rotation of Q and K.

track_changes Practical Workflow: For original Transformer: add sinusoidal PE to input embeddings (element-wise addition). For modern LLMs: use RoPE (rotary) which is applied to Q and K projections, not to the embeddings. This decouples position from content and enables length extrapolation.
sections.codeBlock:
# Sinusoidal positional encoding
class SinusoidalPositionalEncoding(nn.Module):
    def __init__(self, d_model, max_len=5000):
        super().__init__()
        pe = torch.zeros(max_len, d_model)
        position = torch.arange(0, max_len, dtype=torch.float).unsqueeze(1)
        div_term = torch.exp(torch.arange(0, d_model, 2).float() *
                            -(math.log(10000.0) / d_model))
        pe[:, 0::2] = torch.sin(position * div_term)
        pe[:, 1::2] = torch.cos(position * div_term)
        pe = pe.unsqueeze(0)  # [1, max_len, d_model]
        self.register_buffer('pe', pe)

    def forward(self, x):
        # x: [B, T, d_model]
        return x + self.pe[:, :x.size(1)]

# Visualize frequency spectrum
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
d_model = 512
pos = torch.arange(100)
div_term = torch.exp(torch.arange(0, d_model, 2).float() * -(math.log(10000.0) / d_model))
# Dimensions 0, 1: high frequency (fast oscillation)
# Dimensions 100, 101: medium frequency
# Dimensions 254, 255: low frequency (slow oscillation)
print(f"Dim 0 frequency: 1/{2*math.pi/div_term[0]:.2f} = {div_term[0]/(2*math.pi):.2f} cycles/token")
print(f"Dim 100 frequency: 1/{2*math.pi/div_term[100]:.2f} = {div_term[100]/(2*math.pi):.2f} cycles/token")
# The sinusoidal encoding creates a unique spatiotemporal signature for each position

sections.title: Cross-Attention — How the Decoder Reads the Encoder
sections.description:
psychology Intuition: The decoder's cross-attention layer bridges encoder and decoder. Queries come from the decoder's previous layer, while Keys and Values come from the encoder's final output. This lets each generated token dynamically attend to relevant parts of the input sequence. The mechanism is identical to self-attention, but with different sources for Q vs K/V.

bar_chart Concrete numbers: In translation, cross-attention weights show interpretable alignment patterns: the decoder's first token heavily attends to the encoder's first few tokens, and attention shifts monotonically through the source sequence as generation progresses. For 6 decoder layers, later layers show sharper, more monotonic alignment than early layers. Cross-attention accounts for ~25% of decoder FLOPs (self-attention: 25%, cross-attention: 25%, FFN: 50%).

search Interview Angle: "Why did decoder-only models drop cross-attention?" Two reasons: (1) For text generation (vs translation), there's no encoder output — the model generates from a prompt. (2) Encoder-decoder makes it hard to scale — bidirectional encoder attention requires full sequence visibility, which limits batch size and increases memory. Decoder-only with causal attention is simpler and scales better. For tasks that benefit from bidirectional context (summarization, classification), encoder-decoder may still win, but decoder-only models like GPT-4 show comparable quality with enough scale.
sections.codeBlock:
# Cross-attention visualization (conceptual)
def cross_attention_weights(decoder_hidden, encoder_output, layer_idx=0):
    """
    decoder_hidden: [B, T_dec, d_model]
    encoder_output: [B, T_enc, d_model]
    Returns alignment weights: [B, n_heads, T_dec, T_enc]
    """
    n_heads = 8
    d_k = decoder_hidden.size(-1) // n_heads

    # Q from decoder, K/V from encoder
    Q = decoder_hidden.view(B, -1, n_heads, d_k).transpose(1, 2)
    K = encoder_output.view(B, -1, n_heads, d_k).transpose(1, 2)

    scores = Q @ K.transpose(-2, -1) / math.sqrt(d_k)
    weights = F.softmax(scores, dim=-1)
    return weights  # Shows decoder→encoder alignment

# ponytail: real alignment is learned, not pre-specified.
# The model discovers cross-lingual alignments without any
# explicit alignment supervision — emergent property of attention.

sections.title: Label Smoothing & Training Tricks
sections.description:
psychology Intuition: The original Transformer used several training innovations that became standard. Label smoothing replaces hard target distributions (one-hot) with soft targets: P_target(k) = (1-ε) × δ_{k,y} + ε / V, where V is vocabulary size and ε=0.1. This prevents the model from becoming overconfident and improves generalization.

bar_chart Concrete numbers: Label smoothing (ε=0.1) improves BLEU by ~0.5 points on WMT tasks. It reduces the gap between teacher-forcing and inference-time performance by ~20%. The warmup + decay learning rate schedule (inverse square root: lr = d_model^(-0.5) × min(step^(-0.5), step × warmup^(-1.5))) was critical for training stability — warmup for 4000 steps (lr from 0 to 7e-4 for Base), then decay as 1/√step. Learning rate too high without warmup → training diverges within first 100 steps.

track_changes Practical Workflow: For training a Transformer: use Adam (β₁=0.9, β₂=0.98, ε=1e-9), inverse square root LR schedule with 4000-8000 warmup steps, label smoothing ε=0.1, dropout=0.1 on attention weights + FFN + embeddings, residual dropout=0.1. For modern LLMs: swap to AdamW, cosine schedule with warmup (typically 1-5% of total steps), and gradient clipping at 1.0.
sections.codeBlock:
# Label smoothing
class LabelSmoothing(nn.Module):
    def __init__(self, vocab_size, smoothing=0.1):
        super().__init__()
        self.smoothing = smoothing
        self.confidence = 1.0 - smoothing
        self.vocab_size = vocab_size

    def forward(self, logits, target):
        """
        logits: [B, T, V]
        target: [B, T] (token ids)
        """
        B, T, V = logits.shape
        log_probs = F.log_softmax(logits, dim=-1)

        # Smoothed target: (1-ε) on correct token, ε/V everywhere else
        smooth_target = torch.full_like(log_probs, self.smoothing / self.vocab_size)
        smooth_target.scatter_(-1, target.unsqueeze(-1), self.confidence)

        loss = -(smooth_target * log_probs).sum(dim=-1).mean()
        return loss

# Learning rate schedule (Transformer paper)
def inverse_sqrt_lr(step, d_model=512, warmup=4000):
    """Inverse square root LR with linear warmup."""
    return d_model ** (-0.5) * min(step ** (-0.5), step * warmup ** (-1.5))

# Plot: LR peaks at warmup step, then decays
for step in [100, 1000, 4000, 10000, 50000, 100000]:
    lr = inverse_sqrt_lr(step)
    print(f"Step {step:6d}: lr = {lr:.6f}")

sections.title: Encoder-Decoder vs Decoder-Only: The Convergence
sections.description:
psychology Intuition: The field converged on decoder-only (GPT-style) for most applications. Here's why:

Encoder-decoder (original Transformer): Used in translation (T5, BART, mT5). Encoder sees the full input bidirectionally; decoder generates causally with cross-attention to encoder. Pros: better at tasks needing full input understanding (summarization, classification). Cons: 2× the model size for same capacity, harder to scale (cross-attention adds memory), can't do in-context learning naturally.

Decoder-only (GPT-style): Single stack of decoder blocks with causal attention. Pros: simpler architecture, better scaling properties, natural in-context learning (prompt as prefix), efficient inference (single cache, no encoder). Cons: causal masking limits early-token visibility.

bar_chart Concrete numbers: At 1B params, encoder-decoder slightly edges decoder-only on summarization (+1-2 ROUGE). At 100B+, decoder-only matches or exceeds on all tasks. GPT-3 (175B, decoder-only) matched T5 (11B, encoder-decoder) on SuperGLUE despite 16× fewer FLOPs-per-task. The scaling story is clear: decoder-only wins at scale because of architectural simplicity and efficient training/inference.

search Interview Angle: "When would you still choose encoder-decoder?" (1) Translation — the encoder can see both source and partially-generated target for refined understanding. (2) Tasks requiring global context before generation — long-form QA with evidence documents. (3) Multi-modal where one modality informs another (image captioning). For pure text at scale (>10B params), decoder-only is the default choice.
sections.codeBlock:
# Architecture comparison: encoder-decoder vs decoder-only

def encoder_decoder_forward(encoder, decoder, src, tgt):
    """Encoder-decoder: bidirectional encoder + causal decoder with cross-attn."""
    # Encoder: process full source
    encoder_out = encoder(src, mask=None)  # No mask = bidirectional
    # Decoder: causal generation with cross-attention
    causal_mask = torch.triu(torch.ones(tgt.size(1), tgt.size(1)), diagonal=1).bool()
    decoder_out = decoder(tgt, encoder_out, self_mask=causal_mask)
    return decoder_out

def decoder_only_forward(decoder, input_ids):
    """Decoder-only: causal attention only, no encoder."""
    causal_mask = torch.triu(torch.ones(input_ids.size(1), input_ids.size(1)), diagonal=1).bool()
    return decoder(input_ids, mask=causal_mask)

# Param comparison for equivalent quality
# Enc-dec: N = N_enc + N_dec (separate stacks)
# Dec-only: N = N_dec (single stack)
# At equivalent total params, dec-only has ~2x the decoder depth
# -> better at generation tasks
print(f"Enc-dec 6+6 layers: 12 total blocks")
print(f"Dec-only 12 layers: 12 total blocks (all decoder, deeper)")
print(f"At same param budget, dec-only has more generation capacity")
print(f"because all layers participate in generation, not just the decoder")

sections.title: References & Further Reading
sections.description:
• Attention Is All You Need (Vaswani et al., 2017) — The original Transformer paper. Must-read for every ML engineer.
  • Efficient Transformers: A Survey (Tay et al., 2020) — Comprehensive survey of efficient attention variants (sparse, linear, cluster).
  • Universal Transformers (Dehghani et al., 2018) — Recurrent refinement of transformer representations.
  • Reformer: The Efficient Transformer (Kitaev et al., 2020) — LSH attention for O(N log N) complexity.
  • Exploring the Limits of Transfer Learning with a Unified Text-to-Text Transformer (T5, 2019) — Encoder-decoder at scale. Shows the importance of the span corruption objective.
  • GPT-3: Language Models are Few-Shot Learners (Brown et al., 2020) — The paper that cemented decoder-only as the dominant architecture for scaling.

details:
Prerequisites: See Introduction to Neural Networks for foundational concepts. Also see Neural Network Training for optimization fundamentals.

Staff Engineer Summary: The original Transformer paper changed ML more than any paper in the last decade. Its three key insights: (1) Self-attention replaces recurrence with full parallelization — O(1) path length between any tokens. (2) Multi-head attention lets the model learn different relationship types in different subspaces. (3) The encoder-decoder architecture with cross-attention elegantly handles sequence transduction. Modern LLMs evolved to decoder-only because it scales better, but every concept here — scaled dot-product attention, multi-head projections, residual connections, layer normalization, positional encoding — transfers directly. Understanding this paper at the implementation level is the single best investment for FAANG ML interviews.
